speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Bekler, we were talking about religious observance. And do you remember-- could you-- do you remember celebrating the high holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Yeah. sure, sure. Every Yom Kippur, you know, I remember, yeah. Should I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's come every Yom Kippur, you know? My father with the about 12 [INAUDIBLE] before the-- the hour, you know, he went all-- always in shul. Now, every Yom Kippur, you know, he went and he got a kittel. You know what's a kittel, you know? With this, he went there. And I remember he took me there too, and not the best children. And, [? you know, ?] and I went with him, you know? And they pray, you know, the-- this was the high holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we was young, I remember we-- I drink water. I drink with kids, you know, three, five years old. Nobody-- if-- [LAUGHS] that-- this-- and they never give us a drink water. This is-- this is the way every Yom Kipper. Yom Kipper, who wants, you know? I fast. I fast, I remember, my mother give me, you know, in the shul. I went with the father in shul. And she give me a piece of challah with the [? fliegel, ?] you know, chicken there. And-- [LAUGHS] Now, I tell you that [LAUGHS] I don't eat later. Though about 12 o'clock he give me it, you know? I eat. It's not like this when I got bigger, you understand? So-- yeah, I was fasting always. Even now, I am not so religious, you know? Yom Kipper, you see, even I don't eat. It's not religious, no. I-- I-- I fast. Now, the rabbi, Rabbi Menashe, he would come in for the high holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: He'd come in for the most high holidays. He-- it's not the whole holidays, you know? But the rabbi, a lot see them. They got to [NON-ENGLISH]. Now, the mostly he come to high holidays, though is in shtiebel In this shtiebel, what they have are small, you know? It's small.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went over in shul, the [INAUDIBLE]. They all have Seder in shul. Their shul is-- can-- can go a few hundred people in the shul. So they went, yeah, when he was-- many-- many times he was coming there. You mentioned the Hasidim went to visit the-- the rebbe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any occasions where your father or perhaps grandfather traveled to the rebbe for advice?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, my grandfather was there always when he-- my grandpa was with the rabbi together in [INAUDIBLE]. He was in Zawichost, But since he gave him this, he [? got ?] with him, you know, though he was always-- Now, I remember when he was 72 years old. He's coming home to stay with the daughter, with the grandchildren, with this. He told me, they weren't so good. I came home [INAUDIBLE], you know, there. So then he was coming home. Now, before, when he was younger, he used to got, you know-- He used to got, you know, to make paint, paint from stones. And this I-- this is such a public for he burned stones. He got stones. He burned a-- a fire in-- in different stones to make the white-- white paint. He used to got this. He-- he used to got boots, you know, [? he rent. ?] They used to burn this. And after, the son took over. He [INAUDIBLE]. When he died back in '72.

speaker: interviewer
text: The son died in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my-- my grandfather--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --died. Though-- no, the son was together with him, with my zeyde, you know? But this then they make. And after he-- he used to real estate. He used to, my zeyde, you know? But that's not when he went to the rabbi. He gives over always the son.

speaker: interviewer
text: The son, got four son. And the son, they was working there, public in their stall. And they used to go from my city, Zawichost, Ozarow, [INAUDIBLE], Skarzysko, ultimate [INAUDIBLE], and give the-- the stuff that there, you know? And this they got [INAUDIBLE]. This is my-- my mama's brother that she make a living. He used to live in a nice house, you know, used to. What happened? Do you know what happened to the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, he got killed. He was alive when I went away. He was alive. He died. They kill him. Now, a horrible-- Rabbi [INAUDIBLE] was the Rabbi Menashe and Rabbi [INAUDIBLE]. Rabbi [INAUDIBLE], he died. He died before-- yeah, he died before. Now Rabbi-- Rabbi Menashe, he went away. With all, like everybody, they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: In my city was the son, [? Moshe. ?] We went when the war start. All Jewish, they went to market, you know, and traveling with the rabbi. A lot, they won't go away. The rabbi told me, no. Don't-- don't go-- don't-- the rabbi, you know? It's not that good. Even it's coming, you know, nobody believe. And nobody believe it. This is the reason. If [INAUDIBLE] stayed on, nobody went away. Well, how aware were you of events that were taking place in Germany before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know? Before the war, do you know what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. I, of course. I know why I know. But a lot, they-- they come up from Germany in 1938. They told-- they went to Poland. They went all over, you know? But they run away. They know. They told me, it's very bad. Hitler is no good. This is the reason why we run away. German Jewish, you understand? This is the reason there was Kristall night, They told us, you understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: You read them. Yeah, this I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how you felt when you read about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, I'm not a-- I couldn't-- I couldn't find out. Even I wasn't Russian. Why? I was coming home. When I know I not meet nobody from my family, I stay Russian. You know why? Well, I-- I couldn't-- no, I couldn't believe, you know, they all got killed. And I was coming home, you know? Everybody, they told me. They took away from-- they all said that they took away the Jewish, and they killed them not far, eight kilometer [INAUDIBLE], in-- in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: This you found out when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I found out when I was coming 1946. This is the reason I was coming from Russian to see the family. But I couldn't believe. I mean, maybe I find somebody, a sister, a this. And I couldn't find nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit, because I'm going to ask you about that, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your after-war experiences. But do you remember what you did and how you felt on September 1, 1939 when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, yeah. My mother told me, [? Shmil, ?] you see, it's like this. I should go away. And all the children, they was in Lodz, you know? When the war start, they was coming back from Lodz to the mother. We was all together, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And after, my mother told me, [? Shmil, ?] you got to stay away. You go away, you know? I tell you, no. Where I can't leave you. No, no. Now, after my friends coming, [? Shmil, ?] you go away. You see what they took? They-- they take-- they I-- I work. Where did they say that you should go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should go away from the-- some-- somewhere else, you know? We don't know. We don't know, you know? The make [INAUDIBLE] Russian this. We don't know. We just run away when-- when it was very bad there, [?you know?] ? And-- and I was there two weeks, I think, three weeks. I-- I don't remember. They took us to work, the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Nazis first come into Zawichost?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September. 1st September, the 2nd September.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were there on--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --September 1?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was the 2nd, the 3rd, about two, three weeks. After they took a woman in this to work. We work, you know? This-- the streets, you know, the streets. Everything's down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the reason we run away. This was in the night, I remember, about two-- two weeks, three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ran away two weeks after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, after this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the first two weeks. How did life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the life changed. The two weeks when I run away on the road, I saw on the road, a lot run away, you understand? And the Germans with the 36 planes, you know, they shot off the people, you know, with-- with [INAUDIBLE], you know? They-- and a lot they kill horses. That make many violence, you know? And very bad, very bad. All-- all the road, all the road you should have seen where they killed them all-- all from-- from the plane. And-- and they shot them. In we were-- and I-- I went away. I went to [INAUDIBLE], Skarzysko-- not Skarzysko, [? Krasnik, ?] Lubelski. We all stayed over there, Janow Lubelski you know, in-- in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We running. [INAUDIBLE]. But-- but the rabbi they was-- we was coming Friday night, I remember. They used to give us challah and fish. And we-- and we run away. And a lot-- a lot is sitting in Janow Lubelski you know? But we don't know where-- where we went. We run. So you don't know where. You just ran?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just like clothes. I got nothing, with nothing. I got a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do run away with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you run away with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With friends, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three friends, three friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- now, that was on the road a lot. We-- we meet. We all together, you understand? You see. I don't know that I don't know three friends. I got good friends when I was in school, in-- in-- in cheder, you know? I went-- I have the pictures tucked in the-- and we run away. One got killed on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rest-- the rest of your family, your-- your younger brother and your sister stayed home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left home. They stayed home.

speaker: interviewer
text: They stayed home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother, you forced me, you know? Go [? Shmil, ?] go. Go, go, go. And I run. And after, let me tell you, I won't go back. I-- I was crying. I was about 17 years old. I was crying, and I won't go back. I-- I-- I told myself, I-- I live. My mother was sick, you know, she-- very nerves with everything, you know? And I won't go back. I couldn't go back no more. Was fire all over, you understand. And I-- we went. We went till we was [INAUDIBLE]. I remember after. Oh, it was Janow [INAUDIBLE]-- Janow, yeah. And after Krasnik, Krasnik I got family. They give me a-- a few dollars. I should go away. And they went too, other places. And when I went to [INAUDIBLE] Ukraine, [INAUDIBLE], I see it's no planes, no nothing, quiet. And I don't know what this is.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walk. We walking. And a lot, they got horses, buggies, horses, no cars. And they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it took me about two weeks, three weeks. About three weeks it took, four weeks till we was coming. And now I see quiet. After, I find out the Russian there. It's a lot, a lot got the uniform, you know, the-- the uniform in Polish, yeah. And the Russian killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish-- Polish were killed. Polish [? were ?] [? there ?] when the Russian-- when they run away too, the Polish. You understand? I don't know if it's all Jewish. It's not Jewish there that was in the Polish army. No, they run away too, you understand? And they-- and the Russian killed them, the [INAUDIBLE] what they went there-- there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those clothes, Polish clothes, you know, with the this. After a lot, they went to the Jewish. They give them a jacket. They change. They change this when it happened this. And-- and-- and I was in there in [INAUDIBLE] Ukraine, Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Russian-- in Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this Russian-occupied area--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --protect. Did you hide your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't necessary.

speaker: interviewee
text: They was all Jewish. They was Jewish. You know, I-- I went-- there brothers there was. And, you know, I told me, I [INAUDIBLE] run away from home. He took me inside. [INAUDIBLE] [? Michael ?] [INAUDIBLE]. I never forget, all brothers. And they told me, maybe you work for me. He used to sell, you know, wheat for bread, you know, flour, everything, you know? I used to work for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after, every [INAUDIBLE] they call [INAUDIBLE], you know, they must register. And no, you must. So I register to go home. Who wants to go home? They can go home. No, I register. I will go home. You know, when I registered to go home, that they send me to Siberia, Siberia. Why did you-- were you picked to go to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they pick.

speaker: interviewer
text: They--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances that they picked you, just random?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They picked--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every-- everybody who registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I want go home. I want go home to my mother, you know? I know I left mother, you know. That mean I couldn't go. I couldn't go. That why I registered, to go home. But they said that's to Siberia in a coal mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Novosibirsk, Novosibirsk in the coal mine, you know? This I can-- I wish my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe those--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish my dead, you know, my dead. We used to work there 18 hours in the coal mine, no food. They got 600 gram bread a day. Is this-- the bread was like a stone, you know? [INAUDIBLE] a little bit, and used to work with a little [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] what there was, a little water with a salt pickle. And this you work 14, 15 hours in the coal mine, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And every day in the coal mine, you know, when they take the coal, they put holes and they put dynamite. Falls down and the coal, you know, a lot of-- every day, eight people, 10 people got killed in the coal mine. And I was there 18 months in the coal mine. I tell you, no, hunger-- hunger in this. I tell you, now, I must go in the army, kill a few Germans, you know? [? And they ?] [? come ?] them in like this. And took me, you know, long, from eight months, you know? And this was every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, every day. And I used to work, you know? And hunger, I remember I went to the doctor, you know? I couldn't work, to do the work, you know? I couldn't work, and I couldn't work, you know? They hit me, this. I should. But I couldn't, no, till a half an hour took, you know? I got this and they, I used to.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was a [INAUDIBLE]. You know what's [INAUDIBLE]? I used to carry big bins. Then they take the coal, you know? They hold it there, you know? Now, there was gas. There was this-- I-- I think in mine, my-- mine-- my life is-- is all dead together, you understand? It's better to go in the army, you know? So I wrote [INAUDIBLE] [? Lenin. ?] The [INAUDIBLE] have [? Lenin. ?] I want go in the-- in the army. Well, how did you have the opportunity to enlist in the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you work 15 or 18 hours, and they told us. Who wants to go to the army can go to the army. [? That ?] I wrote because I have [? Lenin, ?] you know? Took three months. They said I should go to [? Beilsk ?] in the-- in the bunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they learned after-- [? automatic-- ?] the rifle, you know? And I went there in 1942. I remember they send me. And stay-- in state Stalingrad I went. I was in Stalingrad, in [? Beilsk, ?] I learned. And after we learned, you know, I was an engineer-- engineer pontoon in [? bridgen, ?] you know? I bet that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to build-- We used to build on the Volga, water, the bridges, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pontoons. Pontoons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pontoons, you know? We built. They [INAUDIBLE] went. The tanks went. And after we take apart. And we built another.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name-- do you remember the name of the unit that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: 65-- 65 Pontoon-- Pontoon Engineer. Yeah, 65 [INAUDIBLE], this I remember. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And the work you were doing were to build the pontoons?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there-- and there was working, no. There, I-- I-- I not care find my [? bed. ?] Food, I got. What got every day [LAUGHS] you should have seen it. [? Spilt, ?] they give. You know what [? spilt, ?] huh? This was real [INAUDIBLE], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and after this, when they built the bridges in the night, you know, we cut woods, the woods for the-- for the bridges. It was working day and night. Now, there was food and-- and this. And I don't care for the dead. You know, a lot got killed when they built. You should have seen the planes, the German planes, you know? So where was this? Which battles were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalingrad, this. This was in Stalingrad, yeah. Oh. [SIGH] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in the Russian army, did they-- the people you worked with-- did they know of your Jewish identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Jewish. Yeah, they know. I told them, [INAUDIBLE]. I never there. Then I-- I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when I was-- there was, yeah, in the coal mine, Ukrainian, not Russian, Ukrainian. But they was in Russia. They was big anti-Semitism. They-- he killed so many in there-- in the coal mine. But in coal mine, you don't know if they're killed. I mean, he killed-- [INAUDIBLE] are his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: He told me many times, now, I was a good worker. He told me, you [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] you know, this is not your [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Somebody works, you-- you eat, you know? This is a [INAUDIBLE]. Other working, you [INAUDIBLE]. You-- you eat. [LAUGHS] Oh, yeah. There was-- no, you see, by [? Stalin, ?] maybe he was a Communist. No, there was the Russian people alone was good people. A lot of times how much they give me a potatoes, you know? I used to do a job for them, you know, cutting hay, for the-- this. [? But ?] a Russian, every family, they got the-- a cow, chickens, you understand? And then this was. But there was a-- [INAUDIBLE], you know? They was good. Russian people was good. But the government [INAUDIBLE], you know? Even there was election, I remember. And I-- I-- I couldn't work. I never understand. This was a Russian. While you were there, did you maintain your observance of Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There never was. When I was there, I was looking all over. They all closed. Old synagogues, big synagogues there was, all closed, all-- there is nothing, now nothing. Even I find, I remember, there was after the war, yeah, after the war, I find such a religious people, you know? We got see this, you know, with peace. This after the war, with-- I went there. Then-- I-- I find.

speaker: interviewer
text: They got [? shtiebel, ?] you know? And-- how you say-- nobody know they [? daven, ?] they-- you understand? What kind-- this was in-- in [INAUDIBLE], in [INAUDIBLE], this. But there was religious Jewish. A lot, they got [INAUDIBLE], you know? They got everything, you know? No, this was for the government. They was afraid for the government. But they got the-- So while you were in the Russian army, you encountered Jews in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: You encountered-- you met Jews living in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I got a-- a [INAUDIBLE], a general. He was a Jew. He was-- yeah, you know? He so-- was so good, you know? He told me, no, this is-- he gave me a lot of bread, of this, yeah. A lot, [INAUDIBLE] was in the Russian army. But I was in my platoon. Oh, was the gun-- the gunmeister, you know? He was a general or what, very big. He was good to me. Every time he invites me, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- while you were in Russia, did you know what was going on in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. no. There I never--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about news of the war? Was there any news--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you received?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I never know this. This, never, you see? No papers, no radio. We never-- we never believe. When I know, I tell you, when I know, it's like this. I've never come home. I've been saying this. You know why? I used to go with a-- a Jewish girl, and I respect her there after the war, you know? And-- and I used to love.

speaker: interviewer
text: The father was-- how you say-- oh, it's something, nice-- nice family. They had got two sons in the army, you know? Every Friday, they invite me, you know? And she bake challahs. She baked this. I--I-- I was close to. And after was common-- and was common who wants to go to Poland back. But General [? Shakorski, ?] the Polish [? meister, ?] they told, who wants-- who was born in Poland, they can back to Poland. This is the reason I went back to Poland. Why? But I couldn't believe, you know? My family had got killed. Did you go in with the Russian army into Poland toward the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not with the Russian army. I went with the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: But when did your military service end?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946. It ended in 1945. I was in the hospital, I-- yeah. I-- you know, I'm-- I'm wounded twice with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get wounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the-- with the shrapnels, from when the shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back. This was in 1942, 1943, yeah. 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of Russia? Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Stalingrad by the bridges. By the-- where you build the bridges, you know? They shot and-- and I got wounded. This was in 1943 or '44. But I was in hospital in Stalingrad. The ended till the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After, when-- when I-- when they release me, no-- no more war, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you given a medal because you were wounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the, you know, a passport to go in restaurants, not won medal, to restaurants to eat there, you know, to eat their lunch. This I got till I went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went home, you were already out of uniform, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --returned as a civilian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, will, yeah. This I went home. I couldn't believe, you-- I couldn't believe, you know? I can find nobody. You see, this is the reason I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you returned directly to Zawichost?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, directly to Zawichost. And when I was coming up the [INAUDIBLE] in Zawichost, no Jewish, no-- no nobody. Polack, though, they told me though I ask here for my teachers, you know? Public school, I remember, you know, Sabatowski, you know? They told me, yeah, he's alive. And they told me where he lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was living on other side. And I went to him. I went to him. He told me such a nice-- you see? There is a lot of Polacks. Now, he was so good. Give me an eating. Give me money. He told me, [? Shmulik, ?] go [INAUDIBLE] where-- there is pogrom. There was-- when I was there in 1946, there was the pogrom in Kielce. They killed 150 people. You understand? That was the time you were in Zawichost?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first time I was in Zawichost, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any actions against Jews in Zawichost at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't stay. I was a day there. I was bad. But he not let me go. But there was such a-- but they see a Jew, you understand, they kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very dangerous--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you to be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very dangerous. This is the reason he gave me money. He told me to go away. Where I went, I went Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before we get there let me ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you discover what happened to your family? Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody told me there, you know? Somebody told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he described what happened in 1943?
